items.mistake: Calling any data processing script "machine learning"
items.fix: Reserve ML for systems whose parameters are fit from data to improve a measurable task performance—not fixed SQL aggregates or one-off charts.
items.why: Blurring the term hides whether a system actually generalizes or merely reports historical numbers.
items.example: A dashboard that sums daily revenue is analytics; a model that predicts next-week revenue from past features is ML.

items.mistake: Training and evaluating on the same data
items.fix: Hold out validation and test sets before training; report metrics only on data the model did not see during fitting.
items.why: Training accuracy on memorized rows is a useless proxy for real-world performance.
items.example: 99% accuracy on train but 52% on test usually means the model learned quirks of the training file, not the task.

items.fix: Document what each column or signal means, how it is computed, and what the label represents before choosing an algorithm.
items.why: Wrong features or leaky labels make any algorithm look good in training and fail in production.
items.example: Using "account closed date" as a feature to predict churn when that date is only set after a user churns.

items.mistake: Expecting ML to work with almost no data
items.fix: Match model complexity to dataset size; use simpler models, regularization, or more data collection when examples are scarce.
items.why: High-capacity models need sufficient examples to learn stable patterns instead of noise.
items.example: A deep network on 200 labeled images often overfits; logistic regression or data augmentation may be required first.
